story.timeline_note: Part of the Tenth Canto, during Krishna's pastimes
story.comic_potential: The vivid description of the Lord's form and the devotees' emotional response could be depicted with dramatic visuals.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is served by various divine personalities and energies, indicating His supreme position.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the integration of all energies (vidyā, avidyā, śakti, māyā) in service to the Supreme, reflecting the yogic principle of unifying all aspects of existence in devotion.

teachings.teaching: True devotion leads to ecstatic love, manifesting as physical symptoms like standing hairs and tears.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that such external signs are natural outcomes of internal Kriya-Yoga practice, where prāṇa and mind are united in devotion, leading to spontaneous bodily reactions.

chapter_summary: Akrūra offers a profound prayer to Kṛṣṇa, acknowledging Him as the supreme cause of all causes, the primordial person Nārāyaṇa. He describes how all elements, senses, and deities originate from Him, yet they cannot comprehend His true nature. Akrūra explains various paths of worship—Vedic sacrifices, yoga, knowledge, and devotion—all ultimately leading to the same Lord. He then enumerates Kṛṣṇa's cosmic form, where the universe is His body, and lists His avatāras: Matsya, Hayagrīva, Kūrma, Varāha, Narasiṃha, Vāmana, Paraśurāma, Rāma, Vāsudeva (with Saṅkarṣaṇa, Pradyumna, Aniruddha), Buddha, and Kalki. Akrūra confesses his own ignorance and bondage to māyā, seeking refuge at Kṛṣṇa's lotus feet for liberation.

story.title: Akrūra's Prayer to Kṛṣṇa
story.arc: Akrūra, having arrived in Vraja, offers a heartfelt prayer to Kṛṣṇa, acknowledging His supremacy and seeking refuge.
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs during Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Vraja, before the great battle of Kurukṣetra.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's vivid descriptions of Kṛṣṇa's cosmic form and avatāras could be visually stunning, with dynamic imagery of the universe as His body.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate cause of all causes is Nārāyaṇa, from whom Brahmā and the universe originate.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this points to the realization of the Supreme Self as the source of all prāṇa and consciousness, the goal of yogic absorption.

teachings.teaching: The elements, senses, and deities are manifestations of the Lord, yet they cannot know His true nature.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga teaching that the mind and senses are instruments, but the true Self is beyond them; one must transcend the guṇas through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: All paths of worship—Vedic sacrifices, yoga, knowledge, and devotion—ultimately lead to the same Supreme Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga integrates all paths: karma (action), jñāna (knowledge), and bhakti (devotion) are harmonized through the practice of prāṇāyāma and meditation on the Lord.

teachings.teaching: Even those who worship other deities unknowingly worship Kṛṣṇa, as all rivers flow into the ocean.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga principle of unity: all spiritual practices converge on the same ultimate reality, and the yogi sees the one Self in all.

teachings.teaching: The three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) bind all beings from Brahmā down to the immovable.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that through prāṇāyāma and meditation, one can transcend the guṇas and realize the pure consciousness beyond them.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the witness of all minds and the substratum of the flow of guṇas, which creates the cycle of birth in different species.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of witnessing the mind's modifications without identification, leading to liberation.

teachings.teaching: The entire universe is the body of the Lord; all elements and beings are His parts.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is realized through meditation on the cosmic form, seeing the macrocosm within the microcosm of the body.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's avatāras descend to protect the righteous and destroy the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views avatāras as divine interventions that also serve as archetypes for the yogi's inner journey, each representing a stage of spiritual evolution.

teachings.teaching: The jīva is deluded by māyā, identifying with the body and possessions, and wanders in the cycle of karma.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga aims to break this identification through self-discipline and meditation, realizing the true Self as distinct from the body-mind complex.

teachings.teaching: One cannot control the mind by oneself; only by taking refuge at the Lord's feet can one attain liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga is a grace-based path: through surrender and the practice of prāṇāyāma, the Lord's grace purifies the mind.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is pure consciousness, the cause of all perceptions, and the supreme refuge.
teachings.lens_note: This is the ultimate realization in Kriya-Yoga: the Self is pure awareness, and by meditating on that, one attains liberation.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama, accompanied by Akrura, arrive in Mathura. They are greeted by the city's beauty and the devotion of its people. Krishna kills a proud washerman and grants liberation to a garland-maker named Sudama, who offers him garlands with devotion.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Enter Mathura
story.arc: Krishna's journey to Mathura and his initial acts of establishing righteousness
story.timeline_note: Immediately after the vision of Vishnu in the Yamuna, Akrura brings Krishna and Balarama to Mathura at dusk.
story.comic_potential: The scene of the washerman's beheading with a hand gesture and the women's frantic rush to see Krishna are visually striking.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's presence purifies even the most mundane places and people.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that the divine energy (kriya) sanctifies the practitioner's body and environment when invoked with devotion.

teachings.teaching: True devotion is unconditional and seeks nothing but service.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the highest bhakti is selfless, as shown by Sudama who asks only for devotion, not material boons.

teachings.teaching: Pride leads to downfall; humility leads to grace.
teachings.lens_note: The washerman's arrogance contrasts with Sudama's humility, reflecting the Kriya-Yoga principle that ego must be dissolved for spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the aftermath of Krishna's granting a boon to a devotee, bestowing prosperity, strength, longevity, fame, and beauty, and then departing with his elder brother.

story.title: Krishna's Boon and Departure
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the boon-giving episode, Krishna leaves with Balarama.
story.comic_potential: A scene of divine blessing and majestic exit.

teachings.teaching: The boon includes material and spiritual blessings: prosperity, strength, longevity, fame, and beauty.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these represent the fruits of disciplined practice: inner wealth (prosperity), willpower (strength), sustained effort (longevity), recognition (fame), and harmony (beauty).

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama enter Mathura, encounter the hunchbacked woman Trivakra who offers them sandalwood paste, and Krishna straightens her body. They then proceed to the royal bow, break it, and kill the guards. After witnessing the city's opulence, they return to their camp. Meanwhile, Kamsa, hearing of the bow's destruction and the guards' death, becomes terrified and sees many bad omens. He organizes a wrestling festival, inviting Nanda and other cowherds. The chapter ends with the wrestlers assembling.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama in Mathura
story.arc: Preparation for Kamsa's overthrow
story.timeline_note: After the bow-breaking incident, Kamsa organizes the wrestling festival the next day.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful interaction with Trivakra and the dramatic bow-breaking scene.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's touch can transform and elevate even the most humble and deformed.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes how divine grace (kriya) straightens the crooked mind (vrittis) and awakens spiritual beauty.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is not bound by social norms; He accepts offerings from all with love.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that true devotion transcends external purity; the Lord accepts the heart's offering.

teachings.teaching: Fear and bad omens precede the destruction of the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the inner turmoil (durnimittani) that arises when one is about to transcend ego (Kamsa).

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama, having heard the arena's commotion, approach the entrance where they encounter the elephant Kuvalayapida. Krishna defeats the elephant, kills it, and uses its tusk as a weapon. They enter the arena, and the crowd marvels at them. The people discuss Krishna's divine nature and past deeds. Canura, the wrestler, challenges them to a match, and Krishna responds appropriately.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Enter the Arena
story.arc: Kamsa's downfall
story.timeline_note: After the defeat of various demons, Krishna and Balarama arrive at the arena for the wrestling match.
story.comic_potential: High; the elephant fight and the crowd's reaction are visually dramatic.

teachings.teaching: One should act in accordance with dharma, pleasing the king and all beings, as the king is the embodiment of all beings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of harmonizing individual action with the cosmic order (dharma) and serving the divine in all.

teachings.teaching: Even in competition, one should maintain fairness and avoid adharma.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on righteous action (kriya) and purity of intent.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama defeat and kill the wrestlers Canura, Mustika, Kuta, Sala, and Tosala in the arena of Kamsa. Kamsa orders the arrest of Vasudeva, Nanda, and Ugrasena, but Krishna leaps onto the royal dais, grabs Kamsa by the hair, and throws him to the ground, killing him. The eight brothers of Kamsa attack and are slain by Balarama. The women of Kamsa's family lament. Krishna and Balarama then release their parents Devaki and Vasudeva from bondage and bow to them.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Kill the Wrestlers and Kamsa
story.arc: Kamsa's downfall and the liberation of Devaki and Vasudeva
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs during the wrestling match at Kamsa's arena, immediately after Krishna and Balarama have entered the city of Mathura.
story.comic_potential: High: The wrestling match, the dramatic leap onto the dais, and the killing of Kamsa are visually striking and action-packed.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects the righteous and destroys the wicked; even in a seemingly unjust situation, divine justice prevails.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of karma and the necessity of action (kriya) to restore dharma. The wrestlers and Kamsa represent the ego and negative tendencies that must be overcome through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' devotion is the highest form of love; they see the Lord in all activities and are blessed by his presence.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes constant remembrance of the divine (smarana) as a key practice. The gopis exemplify this by seeing Krishna in every aspect of their daily lives.

teachings.teaching: One should not associate with those who support adharma; a wise person avoids a gathering where injustice prevails.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga teaching of satsanga (association with truth) and avoiding environments that reinforce negative habits.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama return to Mathura, reunite with their parents Vasudeva and Devaki, and console them. They install Ugrasena as king, welcome back the Yadava clans, and send Nanda and the gopas back to Vrindavan. They undergo the sacred thread ceremony with Garga as teacher, then study under Sandipani in Avanti, learning all scriptures in 64 days. As guru-dakshina, they retrieve Sandipani's son from the ocean demon Panchajana and from Yama's city, restoring him to life.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama's Return to Mathura and Education
story.arc: Krishna's early life in Mathura and education under Sandipani
story.timeline_note: After Kamsa's death, Krishna and Balarama return to Mathura, reunite with parents, then go to Avanti for education, and return to Mathura.
story.comic_potential: The retrieval of the guru's son from the ocean and Yama's city has high adventure and divine intervention.

teachings.teaching: One must serve parents and elders; failing to do so leads to suffering after death.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of karma-yoga and selfless service as a means to purify the mind and attain liberation.

teachings.teaching: A guru's instruction should be followed with devotion; the guru-dakshina is a sacred duty.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga highlights the guru as the embodiment of divine grace; fulfilling the guru's request is a form of surrender and accelerates spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's lila as a human conceals his divinity, teaching humility and the value of human relationships.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as a lesson in maya: the divine play teaches that ultimate reality is beyond appearances, and devotion transcends form.

chapter_summary: Uddhava is sent by Krishna to Vrindavan to console Nanda, Yashoda, and the gopis who are suffering from separation. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message, describing his divine nature and assuring them of his return. The chapter ends with the gopis' morning activities and their curiosity about Uddhava's arrival.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
story.arc: Krishna's separation and Uddhava's consolation
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, before his return to Vrindavan.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' morning activities and their curiosity about Uddhava's chariot.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial and equal to all, without favorites or enemies.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the state of non-dual awareness where the practitioner sees the same consciousness in all beings.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's incarnations are for the protection of the righteous and for pastime.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that divine actions are spontaneous (lila) and not bound by karma, inspiring practitioners to act without attachment.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the inner self of all beings, the ultimate cause and sustainer.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of realizing the inner Self as identical with the universal consciousness.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits the gopis of Vrindavan as a messenger from Krishna. The gopis express their intense separation and love for Krishna, questioning his faithfulness. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message, which includes philosophical teachings on the nature of the self and the importance of constant remembrance. The gopis, though comforted, remain immersed in thoughts of Krishna.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to the Gopis
story.arc: Krishna's separation and the gopis' devotion
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, Uddhava is sent to console the gopis.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' dramatic lamentations and their sharp rebukes of the bee (as a metaphor for Krishna's messenger) have a theatrical, almost comedic intensity.

teachings.teaching: The self (ātman) is pure consciousness, distinct from the body and mind, and is unaffected by the three states of waking, dreaming, and deep sleep.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes direct realization of the self through inner stillness, transcending the modifications of the mind.

teachings.teaching: Constant remembrance of Krishna (or the divine) is the highest path, surpassing all external practices like charity, vows, and austerity.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, continuous inner remembrance (smaraṇa) is a key technique to stabilize the mind in the divine, leading to liberation.

teachings.teaching: Detachment from worldly relationships and possessions is necessary for supreme devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework views renunciation not as physical abandonment but as inner non-attachment, which the gopis exemplify by leaving everything for Krishna.

teachings.teaching: The pain of separation (viraha) can deepen love and lead to spiritual growth.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga sees viraha as a powerful catalyst for turning the mind inward, intensifying longing for the divine.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate goal is to merge with the divine, achieved through constant loving remembrance.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of union (yoga) through sustained inner focus on the divine presence.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits Vrindavan to console the gopis and Nanda's family after Krishna's departure. He stays for several months, singing Krishna's pastimes and alleviating their grief. The gopis' intense love for Krishna is praised, and Uddhava returns to Mathura, conveying their devotion to Krishna.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
story.arc: Krishna's departure and the gopis' separation
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, Uddhava is sent to console the residents of Vrindavan.
story.comic_potential: Uddhava's attempt to console the gopis, who are deeply absorbed in Krishna's love, could be portrayed with humorous contrast between his philosophical detachment and their emotional intensity.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' love for Krishna is the highest form of devotion, surpassing even the desires of great sages.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this exemplifies the pinnacle of bhakti where the practitioner's entire being is absorbed in the divine, transcending all worldly attachments and even scriptural injunctions.

teachings.teaching: Even those who are seemingly unqualified (like the gopis) can attain the highest spiritual perfection through pure devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga is accessible to all, regardless of external purity or social status; the gopis exemplify that sincere love for the divine is the sole requirement.

teachings.teaching: The Lord reciprocates with His devotees according to their love, not their external qualifications.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's teaching that the divine responds to the intensity of the practitioner's inner longing, not ritual correctness.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' renunciation of family and social norms for Krishna's sake is the highest form of detachment.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this represents the ultimate vairagya where one gives up even the most cherished attachments for the sake of union with the divine.

teachings.teaching: The dust of the gopis' feet purifies the three worlds, indicating the power of pure devotees.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that association with advanced practitioners is a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga, as their grace accelerates spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The residents of Vrindavan pray for constant remembrance of Krishna in mind, speech, and body.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga ideal of integrating the divine into every aspect of life, making the entire being an instrument of devotion.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the courtesan's house to fulfill her desire, then goes to Akrura's house. Akrura praises Krishna as the Supreme Lord and describes his avatar to relieve the earth's burden. Krishna sends Akrura to Hastinapura to check on the Pandavas.

story.title: Krishna's Visit to Akrura and the Mission to Hastinapura
story.arc: Krishna fulfills the courtesan's desire, then visits Akrura, who extols him. Krishna then sends Akrura to check on the Pandavas.
story.timeline_note: After Kamsa's death, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The contrast between Krishna's intimate pastimes with the courtesan and his majestic reception by Akrura.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord is accessible to all, even those considered fallen, if they approach with love.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that the practice is not restricted by social status; sincere devotion (bhakti) is the key.

teachings.teaching: The Lord descends to relieve the earth's burden and protect the righteous.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as the cosmic duty of the divine to restore balance, mirrored in the practitioner's inner work to balance the gunas.

teachings.teaching: Saints purify by their mere sight, unlike holy waters or deities made of material elements.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the guru's presence is considered more purifying than external rituals; the inner transformation comes through association.

chapter_summary: Akrūra visits Hastinapura, meets Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Bhīṣma, Vidura, Kuntī, and others. He observes Dhṛtarāṣṭra's partiality and the Kauravas' mistreatment of the Pāṇḍavas. Kuntī laments her plight and prays to Kṛṣṇa. Akrūra advises Dhṛtarāṣṭra to be impartial. Dhṛtarāṣṭra acknowledges his weakness but submits to fate. Akrūra returns to Dvārakā and reports to Rāma and Kṛṣṇa.

story.title: Akrūra's Mission to Hastinapura
story.arc: Pre-Kurukshetra diplomacy and family tensions
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's diplomatic visit, Kuntī's emotional outburst, Dhṛtarāṣṭra's inner conflict.

teachings.teaching: One is born alone, dies alone, and experiences the results of one's actions alone.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes personal responsibility in spiritual practice; no one else can do the inner work for you.

teachings.teaching: Wealth acquired unrighteously is taken away by others; it does not bring lasting happiness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that material pursuits without inner purification lead to bondage; true wealth is spiritual realization.

teachings.teaching: The world is like a dream, illusion, or mental construct; one should remain equanimous and peaceful.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's goal of transcending the mind's illusions through meditation and self-awareness.

teachings.teaching: Surrender to the Supreme Lord as the only refuge from the cycle of birth and death.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, surrender (prapatti) is the culmination of practice, leading to liberation.

chapter_summary: Jarāsandha, king of Magadha, attacks Mathurā with a huge army to avenge his sons-in-law Kaṃsa's death. Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, understanding their divine purpose to relieve the earth's burden, engage in battle. Kṛṣṇa decimates the enemy forces, while Balarāma captures Jarāsandha but is stopped by Kṛṣṇa from killing him, as Kṛṣṇa has further plans. Jarāsandha is released and returns to Magadha. Later, the Yavana king Kālayavana, instigated by Nārada, besieges Mathurā. Kṛṣṇa, to protect the Yadus, builds a fortress city in the sea called Dvārakā.

story.title: Jarāsandha's Attack and the Building of Dvārakā
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's Pastimes in Mathurā and Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: After Kaṃsa's death, Jarāsandha attacks Mathurā seventeen times, each time defeated. The eighteenth battle is with Kālayavana, leading to the construction of Dvārakā.
story.comic_potential: High; the repeated battles with Jarāsandha and the strategic building of a fortress city have dramatic and humorous elements.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to relieve the earth's burden and protect the righteous.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of divine intervention to restore cosmic balance through action (kriya).

teachings.teaching: A true hero does not boast but demonstrates prowess through action.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that kriya-yoga is about silent, effective action without ego.

teachings.teaching: One should not kill a captive enemy if there is a higher purpose.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the concept of strategic non-violence (ahimsa) in service of a greater good, a key aspect of kriya-yoga.

chapter_summary: Krishna establishes his capital Dvaraka, receiving divine gifts from the gods, and then goes out unarmed to meet the approaching army.

story.title: Establishment of Dvaraka and Divine Gifts
story.arc: Krishna's earthly pastimes
story.timeline_note: After Krishna settled in Dvaraka, the gods offered gifts.
story.comic_potential: Krishna going out unarmed with a garland, surprising the enemy.

teachings.teaching: The divine gifts (Sudharma assembly, Parijata tree, horses, treasures) symbolize that a realized soul receives all necessities without effort.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that when one is established in the Self (Krishna), the universe provides support spontaneously.

teachings.teaching: Krishna goes unarmed, showing that true strength is spiritual, not material.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga leads to inner fearlessness; one need not rely on external weapons.

chapter_summary: Mucukunda, a great king of the Ikshvaku dynasty, had been guarding the gods in a cave for ages. After being granted a boon, he chose to sleep. The Yavana king, pursuing Krishna, entered the cave and kicked Mucukunda, who then burned him with his angry glance. Krishna revealed himself to Mucukunda, who offered prayers and received a boon of liberation.

story.title: Mucukunda's Deliverance
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes with the Yadus
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's killing of Kamsa and other demons, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The Yavana's futile chase and Mucukunda's sleepy rage provide humorous contrast to Krishna's serene presence.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's names and forms are infinite and cannot be fully enumerated.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the divine is beyond intellectual grasp; one must experience through practice.

teachings.teaching: Material attachment leads to suffering; one should seek the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views detachment as essential for inner peace; the world is a trap for the ignorant.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects His devotees and grants liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that sincere devotion and surrender lead to divine grace and ultimate freedom.

chapter_summary: King Mucukunda, after being awakened by Krishna, recounts his past life as a king who enjoyed worldly pleasures but ultimately found them unsatisfying. He expresses his desire for liberation and devotion to Krishna, rejecting material boons. Krishna praises his steadfast devotion and grants him unwavering bhakti, instructing him to continue his kingly duties while remaining detached, and promises him liberation in a future life as a brahmana.

story.title: Mucukunda's Liberation
story.arc: Mucukunda, disillusioned with worldly life, seeks refuge in Krishna and receives the boon of unwavering devotion and eventual liberation.
story.timeline_note: After the battle with Kalayavana, Krishna meets Mucukunda in a cave.
story.comic_potential: Mucukunda's lament about the futility of royal pleasures and his transformation into a devoted sage.

teachings.teaching: Worldly pleasures and royal power are transient and lead to suffering; true happiness lies in devotion to God.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this echoes the need to transcend sensory attachments through disciplined practice, recognizing the impermanence of material gains.

teachings.teaching: Association with saints (sat-sanga) leads to devotion and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the importance of satsang as a catalyst for inner transformation, aligning with the verse's teaching.

teachings.teaching: A true devotee does not seek material boons but only unwavering devotion.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga ideal of desireless action and single-minded focus on the divine.

teachings.teaching: Even while performing worldly duties, one can maintain inner detachment and devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that action performed with awareness and dedication to God becomes a form of yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of Rukmiṇī, the princess of Vidarbha, who desired to marry Kṛṣṇa. Her brother Rukmī opposed this and arranged her marriage to Śiśupāla. Rukmiṇī sent a brāhmaṇa messenger to Kṛṣṇa with a secret message, pleading with him to abduct her before the wedding. Kṛṣṇa agreed and later abducted her, defeating the assembled kings.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's marriages and pastimes
story.timeline_note: After Kṛṣṇa's return to Dvārakā following the encounter with Jarāsandha and the marriage of Balarāma to Raivatī.
story.comic_potential: High: Secret love letters, a daring abduction plan, and a dramatic wedding heist.

teachings.teaching: Contentment is the greatest wealth; even a poor but content person sleeps happily, while a discontented king suffers.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, contentment (santoṣa) is a key niyama. This verse emphasizes that inner peace comes from being satisfied with what one has, not from external acquisitions.

teachings.teaching: The brāhmaṇa who is content with his lot and devoted to dharma is worthy of reverence.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights the importance of the guru and the brahminical ideal of simplicity and devotion. This verse underscores the value of humility and service to such sages.

teachings.teaching: A king who protects his subjects and ensures their happiness is dear to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of selfless action (karma-yoga) performed as service to the divine, where leadership is a form of seva.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's qualities and form captivate the heart and remove all distress.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, meditation on the divine form (rūpa-dhyāna) is a powerful practice to still the mind and attain inner peace. Rukmiṇī's words exemplify this.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the only suitable match for a virtuous soul; no worldly king can compare.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching reinforces the concept of śaraṇāgati (surrender) to the divine as the ultimate goal, a core tenet of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: One should not hesitate to take bold action for the sake of dharma, even if it means defying social norms.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary often encourages decisive action in the service of the divine, as seen in Kṛṣṇa's abduction of Rukmiṇī, which was a righteous act to protect her from an unwanted marriage.

chapter_summary: Krishna, having received Rukmini's message, decides to abduct her before her marriage to Shishupala. He travels to Kundina with a brahmana messenger. Meanwhile, Rukmini's father prepares for the wedding, and many enemy kings assemble to prevent Krishna. Rukmini anxiously awaits Krishna, and the brahmana returns to reassure her. Krishna and Balarama arrive and are honored by the king. Rukmini goes to the temple of Ambika (Parvati) to pray, then leaves the temple, ready to meet Krishna.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmini
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs after Rukmini sends a message to Krishna and before the actual abduction.
story.comic_potential: High: romantic tension, political intrigue, and the dramatic arrival of Krishna and Balarama amidst enemy kings.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's determination to fulfill his devotee's desire: He acts swiftly to rescue Rukmini from an unwanted marriage.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'divine intervention through human effort' — Krishna responds to Rukmini's surrender (śaraṇāgati) by taking action, showing that devotion activates grace.

teachings.teaching: Rukmini's unwavering faith and prayer: She prays to Ambika for Krishna as her husband, demonstrating single-minded devotion.
teachings.lens_note: This exemplifies 'ekānta-bhakti' (exclusive devotion), a key Kriya-Yoga practice where the mind is fixed on the Divine, leading to inner transformation.

teachings.teaching: The futility of opposing divine will: The enemy kings assemble to prevent Krishna, but their efforts are in vain.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's lens sees this as a reminder that ego-driven actions (like the kings' opposition) are powerless against the flow of divine will (kṛṣṇa-icchā). True strength lies in aligning with dharma.

chapter_summary: Rukmiṇī, adorned with jewels and beauty, walks slowly to the temple, captivating the kings who become infatuated. She glances at them shyly but sees Kṛṣṇa. As she attempts to board her chariot, Kṛṣṇa abducts her in his chariot, defeating the assembled kings, including Jarāsandha, who lament their disgrace.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's marriage to Rukmiṇī
story.timeline_note: Occurs during the svayaṃvara of Rukmiṇī.
story.comic_potential: High; dramatic abduction scene with kings fainting and Kṛṣṇa's swift escape.

teachings.teaching: The divine beauty of Rukmiṇī captivates all, but only Kṛṣṇa is worthy of her.
teachings.lens_note: Illustrates the supremacy of Kṛṣṇa over worldly kings.

teachings.teaching: Kṛṣṇa's actions are irresistible and beyond the control of worldly powers.
teachings.lens_note: Demonstrates divine intervention in human affairs.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama lead the Yadava army against the kings allied with Shishupala and Jarāsandha. After defeating them, Rukmī pursues Krishna to avenge his sister Rukmiṇī's abduction. Krishna defeats Rukmī but spares his life at Rukmiṇī's plea, instead shaving his head and beard as humiliation. Balarama rebukes Krishna for the harsh treatment, then teaches Rukmiṇī about the nature of the self and detachment.

story.title: The Defeat of Rukmī and Balarama's Teaching
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmiṇī and subsequent conflicts
story.timeline_note: Immediately after Krishna abducts Rukmiṇī from her svayamvara.
story.comic_potential: Rukmī's boastful challenge and subsequent humiliation (shaved head) has comedic elements.

teachings.teaching: The self (ātman) is distinct from the body; attachment to the body causes suffering.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the need to transcend bodily identification through meditative practice, realizing the self as pure consciousness.

teachings.teaching: One should not grieve over the inevitable; happiness and sorrow are transient.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework interprets this as a call to equanimity, achieved by stabilizing the mind through Kriya-Yoga, which purifies the subtle energies.

teachings.teaching: Ignorance (avidyā) leads to the cycle of rebirth; knowledge of the self liberates.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga aims to remove avidyā by awakening the dormant spiritual energy (kuṇḍalinī) through disciplined practice.

chapter_summary: After Rukmiṇī's abduction, Śiśupāla, defeated and humiliated, builds the city of Bhojakaṭa and vows not to enter Kuṇḍina until he kills Kṛṣṇa. Meanwhile, Kṛṣṇa marries Rukmiṇī in Dvārakā with great celebration, and the news astonishes neighboring kings.

story.title: Śiśupāla's Vow and Kṛṣṇa's Marriage to Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's marriage to Rukmiṇī and the aftermath of her abduction
story.timeline_note: Immediately after Rukmiṇī's abduction from Kuṇḍina.
story.comic_potential: Śiśupāla's dramatic vow and the grand wedding celebration in Dvārakā.

teachings.teaching: One should abandon mental distress and fix the mind in intelligence, as Śiśupāla did after his defeat.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the practice of pratyāhāra (withdrawal of senses) and dhāraṇā (concentration) to stabilize the mind after a setback.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's marriage to Rukmiṇī symbolizes the union of the individual soul with the divine, celebrated with joy by devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this represents the culmination of bhakti (devotion) and the bliss of samādhi, where the devotee experiences oneness with the divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of Pradyumna, the son of Krishna and Rukmini, who is an incarnation of Kamadeva. As an infant, he is stolen by the demon Shambara and thrown into the ocean, where a fish swallows him. The fish is caught and brought to Shambara's kitchen, where Mayavati (Rati in disguise) discovers the baby. She raises him, and later reveals his identity. Pradyumna learns the Mahamaya vidya, defeats and kills Shambara, and returns to Dvaraka with Rati, reuniting with his family.

story.title: The Return of Pradyumna
story.arc: Pradyumna's abduction, growth in enemy's house, and victorious return
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High: infant thrown into ocean, swallowed by fish, raised by demon's cook, identity reveal, battle with magic, decapitation, aerial return.

teachings.teaching: The divine play (lila) of the Lord includes apparent loss and reunion, demonstrating that nothing is truly lost in the spiritual realm.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'pratyahara' (withdrawal of senses) and 'dharana' (concentration): the soul (Pradyumna) is seemingly lost in material entanglement (Shambara's realm) but through divine grace and inner knowledge (Mayavati's guidance), it re-emerges and conquers illusion.

teachings.teaching: The power of divine knowledge (Mahamaya vidya) destroys all illusions (maya).
teachings.lens_note: Corresponds to 'vidya' or true knowledge in Kriya-Yoga, which dispels the veils of maya and leads to self-realization.

teachings.teaching: One should recognize the eternal relationship with the Divine, even when circumstances obscure it.
teachings.lens_note: Reflects the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on 'sambandha-jnana' (knowledge of relationship) and constant remembrance of the Lord.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of the Syamantaka jewel. Satrajit, a devotee of the Sun god, receives the jewel from Surya. His brother Prasena is killed by a lion while wearing it, and the lion is killed by Jambavan. Krishna is falsely accused of taking the jewel, so he tracks down the truth, fights Jambavan for 28 days, and recovers the jewel. He returns it to Satrajit, who then gives his daughter Satyabhama in marriage to Krishna to clear his name.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel
story.arc: Krishna clears his name of false accusation, recovers the jewel, and marries Satyabhama.
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Krishna's residence in Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: High: mistaken identity, jewel theft, 28-day fight with a bear-king, and a wedding.

teachings.teaching: False accusations can arise even for the innocent; one must act to clear one's name.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the need to purify the mind of false impressions (samskaras) through action (kriya) to reveal the true self.

teachings.teaching: The jewel (Syamantaka) symbolizes material prosperity and divine grace; it brings both blessings and troubles.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views the jewel as the kundalini shakti; when misused, it causes bondage; when offered to the divine, it brings liberation.

teachings.teaching: Surrender and offering of one's best (daughter and jewel) to the divine leads to peace and removal of sin.
teachings.lens_note: This exemplifies the Kriya-Yoga principle of Ishvara-pranidhana (surrender to God) as a means to purify karma.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama visit Hastinapura after the Pandavas' house fire, then return to Dvaraka. Satrajit is murdered by Shatadhanva for the Syamantaka jewel. Shatadhanva flees, pursued by Krishna and Balarama. Krishna kills Shatadhanva but the jewel is missing. Balarama goes to Mithila, Krishna returns to Dvaraka. Akrura and Krtavarma flee in fear. Later, Krishna retrieves the jewel from Akrura and restores peace.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the Pandavas' escape from the lacquer house, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High: chase scene, jewel theft, divine weapons, and a dramatic reveal.

teachings.teaching: Krishna, though omniscient, acts according to social norms to teach humans.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this shows the guru's compassion in descending to the level of the disciple.

teachings.teaching: Greed leads to destruction; Shatadhanva's greed for the jewel causes his death.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga emphasizes detachment from material wealth as a prerequisite for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees; Satyabhama's distress is heard by Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga teaches that sincere prayer (kriya) invokes divine intervention.

teachings.teaching: Even great souls like Akrura may be tested; truthfulness ultimately prevails.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga stresses the importance of truth (satya) in purifying the mind.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the Pandavas in Indraprastha, marries Kalindi (daughter of Surya), and later marries Mitravinda and Satya (Nagnajiti) after subduing seven bulls. The chapter also recounts the burning of Khandava forest and the gifts from Agni and Maya.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages and the Khandava Forest Episode
story.arc: Krishna's visit to Indraprastha and his marriages to Kalindi, Mitravinda, and Satya, with flashback to the burning of Khandava forest.
story.timeline_note: Events occur after the Pandavas' establishment in Indraprastha, before the Rajasuya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: Krishna playfully subduing seven fierce bulls to win Satya's hand, and the humorous confusion of Duryodhana in Maya's assembly hall.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the supreme lord who removes the distress of his devotees (verse 10).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this signifies that the inner Self (Atman) removes the kleśas (afflictions) when one remembers Him constantly.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is self-satisfied and does not need anything from anyone (verse 38).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the yogi, realizing the self as full (pūrṇa), acts without personal desire, like Krishna.

teachings.teaching: One should not beg from a king who follows his own dharma (verse 40).
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the principle of svadharma and non-attachment; in Kriya-Yoga, one should not seek external validation but act from inner fullness.

chapter_summary: Krishna marries several princesses: Satya (daughter of Nagnajit), Bhadra (daughter of Srutakirti), Lakshmana (daughter of the Madra king), and thousands of other women rescued from the demon Bhauma. Arjuna assists in the wedding of Satya by defeating rival kings.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages: Satya, Bhadra, Lakshmana, and the Rescued Women
story.arc: Krishna marries multiple princesses, demonstrating his divine prowess and establishing alliances.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's establishment in Dvaraka and before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Arjuna's lion-like routing of rival kings and Krishna's swooping abduction of Lakshmana at her svayamvara.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's marriages illustrate the principle of divine play (lila) and the integration of worldly duties with spiritual life.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this shows that even the Supreme Being engages in worldly relationships without attachment, teaching householders to perform actions as offerings.

chapter_summary: Krishna, urged by Indra, attacks the demon Naraka (Bhauma) in his fortress Prāg-jyotiṣa. After defeating Mura and his seven sons, Krishna kills Naraka with his discus. The Earth goddess (Bhūmi) offers Naraka's earrings and other treasures to Krishna, who then rescues 16,000 captive princesses, marries them, and brings the pārijāta tree to Dvārakā.

story.title: The Slaying of Naraka and the Rescue of the Princesses
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's marriage to Satyabhāmā and before the Pāṇḍava war.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic battle scenes, divine weapons, rescue of maidens, and a comedic moment where Indra humbly begs for the pārijāta tree.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects His devotees and removes obstacles, as seen when Krishna kills Naraka to retrieve Indra's earrings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the removal of tamas (inertia) through the practice of kriya, allowing the practitioner to attain clarity and divine grace.

teachings.teaching: The Earth goddess Bhūmi's prayer emphasizes that all creation is a manifestation of the one Supreme Lord, and duality is an illusion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the realization of non-duality (advaita) through the purification of the mind in kriya-yoga, where the practitioner sees the divine in all.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's marriage to the 16,000 princesses demonstrates His ability to simultaneously be with each devotee, illustrating the concept of simultaneous immanence and transcendence.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this represents the yogi's ability to experience the divine presence in every moment and every action, maintaining inner unity amidst outer diversity.

chapter_summary: Krishna teases Rukmini about her choice of him as husband, pretending to be unworthy and suggesting she marry a king. Rukmini, hurt, defends her devotion and love, and Krishna consoles her, praising her unwavering faith.

story.title: Krishna's Teasing and Rukmini's Devotion
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, in their private quarters.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful teasing and Rukmini's dramatic reaction create a humorous and tender scene.

teachings.teaching: True devotion is not swayed by external circumstances or words.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of unwavering inner focus (ekāgratā) on the divine, beyond the dualities of praise and blame.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme goal; those who seek him for material gains are misguided.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga aims for liberation (apavarga), not worldly desires. Seeking Krishna for boons is a lower stage.

teachings.teaching: A devotee's love for God is pure and selfless, not based on external qualities.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga ideal of offering actions without attachment to results (niṣkāma karma).

chapter_summary: Rukmini expresses her deep love and devotion to Krishna, recounting how she chose him over other kings and how he rescued her. Krishna responds playfully, and the chapter depicts their intimate conversation and divine pastimes.

story.title: Rukmini's Loving Exchange with Krishna
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as a householder
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, during their early married life.
story.comic_potential: The playful banter and intimate dialogue between divine lovers, with Rukmini's affectionate complaints and Krishna's teasing responses.

teachings.teaching: The Lord reciprocates the love of his devotees in a human-like manner, demonstrating that divine love transcends formalities.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'sahaja' (natural) devotion, where the practitioner's spontaneous love for the Divine is met with a corresponding grace, dissolving ego and fostering union.

teachings.teaching: Even the Supreme Lord, though self-satisfied, engages in worldly roles to teach dharma and the path of devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that the yogi, having realized the Self, may still engage in worldly activities as a 'lila' (divine play) for the upliftment of others, without attachment.

chapter_summary: This chapter lists the sons of Krishna by his chief queens and describes the marriage of Aniruddha to Rocana, the dice game between Balarama and Rukmi, and the subsequent killing of Rukmi by Balarama.

story.title: The Sons of Krishna and the Dice Game
story.arc: Krishna's family and the conflict with Rukmi
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and other queens, their sons are enumerated. Then the marriage of Aniruddha to Rocana is described, followed by the dice game at Bhojakata.
story.comic_potential: The dice game and Balarama's violent reaction to Rukmi's insult provide dramatic and humorous moments.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's queens, despite their beauty and efforts, could not conquer Krishna's mind, showing his supreme detachment.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the state of a yogi who remains unaffected by sensory distractions, maintaining inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger and violence against Rukmi and Kalinga demonstrate the principle of righteous indignation against adharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the kriya-yogin's forceful removal of obstacles on the path, using controlled energy (prana) to overcome ego and injustice.

chapter_summary: King Parikshit asks Shuka about the marriage of Aniruddha to Usha, daughter of Bana, and the ensuing battle between Krishna and Shiva. Shuka narrates Bana's boon from Shiva, his pride, Usha's dream of Aniruddha, Citralekha's help in bringing Aniruddha to Usha, and Bana's discovery leading to a fight where Aniruddha is bound by serpent ropes.

story.title: The Abduction of Aniruddha and the Battle with Bana
story.arc: Aniruddha, grandson of Krishna, is brought by Citralekha to Usha, daughter of Bana. Bana discovers them and binds Aniruddha with serpent ropes, leading to a future battle between Krishna and Shiva.
story.timeline_note: This chapter sets up the conflict that leads to the battle between Krishna and Shiva, which is described in subsequent chapters.
story.comic_potential: High. The dream romance, secret love, magical transport, and dramatic confrontation with a thousand-armed demon king offer vivid visual and emotional scenes.

teachings.teaching: Pride leads to downfall: Bana's arrogance in thinking no one can match him in battle leads to his eventual humiliation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, pride (ahankara) is a major obstacle. Bana's pride in his thousand arms and boon from Shiva blinds him to the divine plan, illustrating how ego binds the soul.

teachings.teaching: Divine love transcends boundaries: Usha's love for Aniruddha, whom she saw only in a dream, shows the power of prema (divine love) that overcomes social and familial constraints.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that true love is a reflection of the soul's longing for the Divine. Usha's dream represents the inner call of the jiva for union with the Supreme.

teachings.teaching: The guru's grace enables the impossible: Citralekha, through her yogic powers, brings Aniruddha to Usha, symbolizing how divine grace (guru-kripa) can unite the seeker with the goal.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the guru acts as a guide who helps the disciple overcome obstacles. Citralekha's yogic journey to Dvaraka represents the inner journey facilitated by the guru.

chapter_summary: Bāṇa, a thousand-armed asura, imprisons Aniruddha. The Vṛṣṇis, led by Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, attack Śoṇitapura. Śiva fights on Bāṇa's side. After a fierce battle, Kṛṣṇa defeats Śiva's forces, cuts off Bāṇa's arms except four, and spares him due to Prahlāda's boon and Śiva's request. Bāṇa releases Aniruddha and his wife Uṣā.

story.title: The Battle with Bāṇāsura
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvāpara Yuga
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Aniruddha's abduction by Uṣā; four months pass before the Vṛṣṇis learn of his captivity.
story.comic_potential: High: cosmic battle with gods, demons, magical weapons, and a fever duel; Kṛṣṇa's mercy and Bāṇa's humiliation provide dramatic and humorous moments.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord is the ultimate refuge; even Śiva and Brahmā surrender to Him.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that all powers are subordinate to the inner Self, and surrender to the Self is the highest practice.

teachings.teaching: One who neglects the Lord's feet in human life is pitiable and cheats himself.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses that human life is for Self-realization; indulgence in sensory pleasures is a waste of the opportunity.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's mercy is unconditional; even a demon like Bāṇa can be saved if he has a devotee's grace.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the power of a guru's (Prahlāda's) blessing, aligning with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on the guru's grace.

chapter_summary: Anu (the son of King Yayati) returns to his capital with his wife, welcomed by citizens and brahmanas, after being blessed by Lord Shiva. The chapter concludes with a verse stating that one who recites this account of Krishna's victory and the battle with Shiva will never face defeat.

story.title: Anu's Return and the Glorification of Krishna's Victory
story.arc: Anu returns to his capital after receiving blessings from Lord Shiva, and the chapter extols the power of remembering Krishna's victory over Shiva.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the battle between Krishna and Shiva, as part of the larger narrative of the Bhagavata Purana.
story.comic_potential: A triumphant return of a king with his queen, accompanied by festive sounds and decorations, followed by a direct address to the reader about the power of remembrance.

teachings.teaching: Remembering Krishna's victory and his battle with Shiva in the morning ensures one never faces defeat.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the power of smaranam (remembrance) as a kriya-yoga practice that aligns the practitioner with divine victory, transforming potential obstacles into spiritual triumphs.

chapter_summary: King Nṛga, a generous king of the Ikṣvāku dynasty, once gave a cow to a brāhmaṇa, but the cow had been lost by another brāhmaṇa. Both claimed it, and Nṛga, unable to resolve the dispute, died and was turned into a lizard for his unintentional misappropriation of brāhmaṇa property. After a long time, Kṛṣṇa lifted him from a well, restoring his divine form. Nṛga praised Kṛṣṇa and ascended to heaven. Kṛṣṇa then taught the Yadus about the severe consequences of stealing brāhmaṇa property.

story.title: The Deliverance of King Nṛga
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Kṛṣṇa's residence in Dvārakā, after the defeat of Bāṇāsura (Chapter 63).
story.comic_potential: High: A king turned into a lizard, rescued by Kṛṣṇa, then a moral lesson about brāhmaṇa property.

teachings.teaching: Misappropriation of brāhmaṇa property, even unintentionally, leads to severe karmic consequences.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the principle of ahimsa and non-stealing (asteya) as essential for purification of the mind and progress in yoga.

teachings.teaching: One should respect brāhmaṇas and never harm them, even if they are at fault.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights humility and surrender to the guru principle, as brāhmaṇas represent the spiritual authority.

teachings.teaching: The grace of the Lord can liberate even a fallen soul who has some merit.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga concept of divine grace (kripa) as the ultimate means to overcome karma.

chapter_summary: Balarama visits Nanda-gokula, meets the gopas and gopis, consoles them with Krishna's messages, stays for two months, enjoys pastimes with the gopis, drinks varuni, and in a fit of intoxication calls the Yamuna river, which disobeys, so he drags her with his plow. She begs forgiveness, and he releases her, then sports in the water. The chapter highlights Balarama's divine power and his affectionate relationship with the Vraja residents.

story.title: Balarama's Visit to Vraja and the Yamuna Incident
story.arc: Balarama's pastimes in Vraja, including his intoxication and subjugation of the Yamuna river.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's return to Mathura and the killing of Kamsa; Balarama visits Vraja during the spring season (Madhu and Madhava months).
story.comic_potential: Balarama's drunken antics, dragging the Yamuna river with his plow, and the gopis' emotional reunion provide vivid, humorous, and dramatic scenes.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' intense love for Krishna is expressed through their lamentation and questioning of his absence.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the stage of viraha-bhakti (devotion in separation), where the soul yearns for union with the Divine, purifying the heart.

teachings.teaching: Balarama consoles the gopis with Krishna's messages, showing the role of the guru or spiritual friend in bridging the gap between the devotee and the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes the necessity of a realized guide (sadguru) who transmits the Lord's grace and teachings to the aspirant.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's intoxication and subsequent actions demonstrate that even divine beings can exhibit human-like behavior, yet their underlying purpose is to display pastimes for the benefit of devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this teaches that the divine descends to the level of the devotee, using all states (including apparent flaws) as teaching tools to attract and instruct.

teachings.teaching: The Yamuna river's submission to Balarama illustrates the supremacy of the divine will over natural forces.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views this as a metaphor for the control of prana (life force) through yogic discipline; the river represents the flow of consciousness that must be directed by the higher self.

chapter_summary: Pauṇḍraka, a foolish king, claims to be Vāsudeva and sends a messenger to Kṛṣṇa demanding he surrender. Kṛṣṇa kills Pauṇḍraka and his ally the king of Kāśī. The son of Kāśī's king, Sudakṣiṇa, performs a black magic ritual to summon a fiery demon, but Kṛṣṇa's Sudarśana cakra destroys the demon and burns Vārāṇasī.

story.title: The Killing of Pauṇḍraka and the Burning of Vārāṇasī
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes as the supreme lord, demonstrating his divinity and protection of devotees.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Balarāma's return to Nanda's village.
story.comic_potential: High: Pauṇḍraka's absurd imitation of Kṛṣṇa, the fiery demon, and the cakra's destruction provide vivid imagery.

teachings.teaching: False claims to divinity lead to destruction.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the danger of ego and false identification with the self, which must be surrendered to the true Self.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and destroys evil.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's lens: The divine cakra represents the power of discrimination that cuts through ignorance and burns away karmic impurities.

teachings.teaching: Hearing about the Lord's exploits purifies one of sins.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga practice of listening to sacred stories to cultivate devotion and inner purification.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about Balarāma's extraordinary deeds. Śuka narrates the story of Dvivida, a powerful monkey friend of Narakāsura, who wreaks havoc across the earth to avenge his friend. He disrupts sacrifices, imprisons people, and finally confronts Balarāma on Raivataka mountain. After a fierce battle, Balarāma kills Dvivida with his fist, praised by celestials.

story.title: The Slaying of Dvivida
story.arc: Balarāma's pastimes
story.timeline_note: After Narakāsura's death, Dvivida seeks revenge.
story.comic_potential: Dvivida's antics, including imitating women and showing his rear, have slapstick humor.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects the righteous and punishes the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of karma: actions have consequences, and divine intervention restores cosmic order.

teachings.teaching: Even a powerful demon cannot withstand the Lord's might.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that ego and pride lead to downfall; surrender to the divine is the path to liberation.

chapter_summary: Samba, son of Jambavati and Krishna, abducts Lakshmana, daughter of Duryodhana, from her svayamvara. The Kauravas capture him. Balarama goes to Hastinapura as a peace envoy but is insulted. Enraged, he uproots the city with his plow and threatens to drown it in the Ganges. The Kauravas surrender, release Samba and Lakshmana, and offer tribute. Balarama forgives them.

story.title: The Abduction of Lakshmana and Balarama's Wrath
story.arc: Krishna's Pastimes
story.timeline_note: Occurs before the Kurukshetra war, during the time of Krishna's earthly pastimes.
story.comic_potential: High: Samba's bold abduction, the Kauravas' overreaction, Balarama's dramatic uprooting of the city, and the comical surrender of the Kauravas.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger is for the sake of instructing the world, not out of hatred or envy.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that divine anger is a teaching tool to correct adharma, not a personal emotion. The practitioner should use discernment to apply force only for dharma.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the sole support of creation, maintenance, and dissolution.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that all actions are ultimately the play of the divine. The practitioner should cultivate detachment by recognizing the Lord as the sole doer.

teachings.teaching: Pride leads to downfall; humility brings protection.
teachings.lens_note: The Kauravas' pride in their power led them to insult Balarama, but their subsequent surrender saved them. In Kriya-Yoga, ego is the greatest obstacle; surrender to the divine is the path.

chapter_summary: After accepting the gifts, Lord Balarama, accompanied by his son and daughter-in-law, returns to Dvaraka. He recounts his exploits among the Yadus in the assembly hall. The text notes that a raised area on the southern bank of the Ganga still marks his feat.

story.title: Balarama's Return and Narration
story.arc: Balarama returns to Dvaraka and recounts his deeds.
story.timeline_note: After the events of the previous chapter, Balarama returns home.
story.comic_potential: Balarama's dramatic narration of his exploits in the assembly hall, with the Yadus reacting in awe.

teachings.teaching: Accepting gifts with grace and then sharing one's experiences with the community is a virtuous act.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of 'sanga' (association) and 'katha' (narrative) as means to integrate spiritual experiences into daily life.

chapter_summary: Narada, curious about Krishna's simultaneous presence in multiple households with 16,000 wives, visits Dvaraka. He witnesses Krishna engaged in diverse activities in different homes—playing dice, bathing, performing sacrifices, teaching, etc.—all at once. Realizing this is yoga-maya, Narada praises Krishna and departs, meditating on his glories.

story.title: Narada's Visit to Dvaraka: The Yoga-Maya of Krishna
story.arc: Narada, having heard of Krishna's feats, goes to Dvaraka to see him. He enters Krishna's palace and is honored. Then, curious about Krishna's simultaneous presence in all his wives' homes, Narada visits each of the 16,000 palaces and sees Krishna engaged in different activities in each. He realizes it is yoga-maya and praises Krishna, who explains he acts to teach humans. Narada leaves, meditating on Krishna.
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to 16,000 wives and the killing of Narakasura.
story.comic_potential: Narada's bewildered expressions as he sees Krishna in different roles in each house, like a cosmic one-man show.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's simultaneous presence in multiple forms demonstrates his yoga-maya, the divine power to be everywhere at once.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'eka-vapuḥ aneka-dharma'—one essence manifesting multiple functions, akin to the unified field of consciousness appearing as diverse experiences.

teachings.teaching: Krishna engages in worldly activities to set an example for householders, showing that one can perform dharma, artha, and kama while remaining detached.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga is not renunciation of action but action with inner detachment. Krishna's varied roles exemplify 'karma-yoga'—acting without attachment.

teachings.teaching: Narada's realization that Krishna's lila is beyond comprehension leads to humility and devotion.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga teaching that intellectual understanding is limited; direct experience through devotion and practice reveals the divine play.

chapter_summary: Krishna's daily routine in Dvaraka, including his morning activities, worship, and court assembly. A messenger from imprisoned kings arrives seeking help against Jarāsandha. Nārada appears and informs Krishna about Yudhiṣṭhira's planned Rājasūya sacrifice. Krishna consults Uddhava.

story.title: Krishna's Daily Routine and the Plea of the Imprisoned Kings
story.arc: Preparation for the Rājasūya sacrifice and the eventual defeat of Jarāsandha
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's return to Dvaraka, before the Rājasūya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's divine morning routine contrasted with the mundane courtly affairs; the dramatic entrance of the messenger and Nārada.

teachings.teaching: The importance of daily spiritual discipline: rising at brahma-muhūrta, meditation, worship, and charity.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes structured daily practice (kriyā) as a means to purify the mind and align with the divine. Krishna's routine exemplifies this.

teachings.teaching: The supreme Lord is the inner controller and the source of all liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees the Lord as the inner Self (ātman) who, through grace, removes ignorance and bondage. Nārada's praise highlights this.

teachings.teaching: Hearing, chanting, and meditating on the Lord purifies even the lowest.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga includes nāma-japa and meditation as key practices for purification.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's pastimes and names are supremely purifying, like the Ganges.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the power of divine names and stories to cleanse the mind.

chapter_summary: Uddhava advises Krishna to kill Jarāsandha to enable the Rājasūya sacrifice, and Krishna agrees. The chapter describes Krishna's journey to Indraprastha, his welcome by the Pāṇḍavas, and his stay there.

story.title: Uddhava's Counsel and Krishna's Journey to Indraprastha
story.arc: Preparation for Rājasūya
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's return from the Pāṇḍavas' exile, before the Rājasūya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic arrival of Krishna's entourage and the joyous reunion with the Pāṇḍavas.

teachings.teaching: The killing of Jarāsandha is necessary for the Rājasūya sacrifice and for the welfare of many.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the removal of obstacles (kleshas) through determined action (kriya) for the higher purpose of self-realization (yajña).

teachings.teaching: One should not refuse a request from brahmins.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of serving others (seva) as a means to purify the mind.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's actions are for the benefit of all beings.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that all actions of the divine are teaching moments (lila) for the aspirant to cultivate detachment and devotion.

chapter_summary: Yudhiṣṭhira expresses his desire to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice. Kṛṣṇa advises him to first conquer all kings. Yudhiṣṭhira sends his brothers to subdue the directions. They bring tribute. Jarāsandha, the powerful king of Magadha, remains unsubdued. Kṛṣṇa, Bhīma, and Arjuna, disguised as brāhmaṇas, go to Girivraja. They challenge Jarāsandha to a duel. Bhīma fights Jarāsandha and, with Kṛṣṇa's hint, splits his body in two. Kṛṣṇa installs Jarāsandha's son Sahadeva as king and releases the imprisoned kings.

story.title: The Slaying of Jarāsandha
story.arc: Preparation for the Rājasūya sacrifice
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile and before the great war.
story.comic_potential: High: Disguised heroes, a duel with a split body, and a dramatic revelation.

teachings.teaching: One should perform righteous acts like the Rājasūya to attain glory and please the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the principle of karma-yoga: performing actions as offerings to the divine, without attachment.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial and grants results according to one's devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the divine responds to the sincerity of practice, not external status.

teachings.teaching: Even an unstable body can be used to attain eternal fame through righteous deeds.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga teaching of using the temporary body for spiritual evolution through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: The Lord provides subtle guidance to his devotees in difficult situations.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is akin to the inner guru guiding the practitioner through intuition during meditation.

chapter_summary: Krishna releases the kings imprisoned by Jarasandha, who praise him and renounce worldly attachments. Krishna instructs them on devotion and dharma, then honors them and sends them back to their kingdoms. He then has Bhima kill Jarasandha and returns to Indraprastha with Arjuna and Sahadeva, where Yudhishthira rejoices.

story.title: Liberation of the Imprisoned Kings
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura and Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Jarasandha's defeat, before the Rajasuya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic release of kings from a mountain cave, their awe at seeing Krishna, and the subsequent battle with Jarasandha.

teachings.teaching: Wealth and power lead to pride and downfall; one should seek refuge in Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this echoes the need to transcend ego and material desires through disciplined practice and surrender to the divine.

teachings.teaching: The world is like a mirage; only the Supreme is real.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes discerning the eternal from the transient, aligning with the teaching to see through Maya.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna leads to liberation from samsara.
teachings.lens_note: This is the core of Kriya-Yoga: cultivating unwavering bhakti through inner discipline.

teachings.teaching: One should perform duties without attachment, focusing on Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's karma-yoga: action as service, not for personal gain.

chapter_summary: Yudhishthira, having heard of Krishna's killing of Jarasandha, praises Krishna as the Supreme Lord. He then performs the Rajasuya sacrifice with many sages and kings present. During the ceremony, Sahadeva declares Krishna as the most worthy of honor. Shishupala objects and insults Krishna, leading to a confrontation where Krishna kills Shishupala with his discus. The sacrifice concludes successfully, and Krishna returns to Dwaraka.

story.title: The Rajasuya Sacrifice and the Death of Shishupala
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as the Supreme Lord
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Jarasandha, Yudhishthira performs the Rajasuya sacrifice. Shishupala's death occurs during the ceremony.
story.comic_potential: High drama: Shishupala's outburst, Krishna's silent tolerance, and the sudden decapitation.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the Supreme Lord, non-dual, and the source of all. Worshiping Him is equivalent to worshiping all beings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the unity of all existence in Krishna, which is realized through selfless action and devotion.

teachings.teaching: One should not insult the Lord or His devotees; doing so leads to downfall.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses the importance of humility and surrender to the divine will, avoiding ego-driven criticism.

teachings.teaching: Constant enmity and meditation on the Lord, even with hatred, can lead to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the power of focused attention (kriya) on the divine, which transforms even negative emotions into a means of union.

chapter_summary: The chapter describes the aftermath of the Rajasuya sacrifice, where all attendees praise Krishna and the sacrifice, then return to their homes. Only Duryodhana, sinful and envious, cannot bear to see the prosperity of the Pandavas. The chapter concludes by glorifying the recitation of Krishna's deeds, including the killing of Shishupala and the liberation of the king.

story.title: The Conclusion of the Rajasuya Sacrifice
story.arc: Rajasuya aftermath and Duryodhana's envy
story.timeline_note: Immediately after the Rajasuya sacrifice of Yudhishthira.
story.comic_potential: Duryodhana's smoldering envy as he witnesses the Pandavas' glory, contrasted with the divine harmony of the sacrifice.

teachings.teaching: Reciting the deeds of Vishnu, such as the killing of Shishupala, liberates one from all sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of kirtana (chanting) as a purifying practice that aligns the practitioner with divine will.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the grandeur of Yudhishthira's Rajasuya sacrifice, the roles assigned to various relatives, the ceremonial bath (avabhṛtha), the distribution of gifts, and the departure of guests. It then narrates Duryodhana's envy upon seeing the opulence, his humiliation due to Maya's illusion in the assembly hall, and his angry departure, which foreshadows future conflict.

story.title: The Rajasuya Sacrifice and Duryodhana's Humiliation
story.arc: The chapter details the successful completion of Yudhishthira's Rajasuya sacrifice, the subsequent celebration, and Duryodhana's envy and humiliation, which sows seeds of future conflict.
story.timeline_note: This event occurs after the Pandavas' exile and before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Duryodhana's mistaken perception of water and land due to Maya's illusion, leading to his fall and Bhima's laughter, has strong visual comedy.

teachings.teaching: The glory of performing a Rajasuya sacrifice with devotion to Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'kriya' as selfless action dedicated to the divine, leading to spiritual elevation.

teachings.teaching: The danger of envy and pride, as seen in Duryodhana's reaction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that envy (mātsarya) is a major obstacle in yoga, binding the soul to material existence.

teachings.teaching: The illusory nature of material perceptions (Maya's illusion).
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga teaching that the world is a projection of the mind; true reality is realized through inner stillness.

chapter_summary: Shalva, a friend of Shishupala, vows to destroy the Yadavas after being humiliated at Rukmini's wedding. He propitiates Lord Shiva and receives a flying city called Saubha, built by Maya Danava. Shalva attacks Dvaraka, causing destruction. Pradyumna leads the Yadava defense, countering Shalva's illusions. During battle, Pradyumna is struck down by Shalva's minister Dyuman but is rescued by his charioteer. Pradyumna rebukes the charioteer for retreating, but the charioteer explains he acted to protect him.

story.title: The Slaying of Shalva
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes: subduing enemies of the Yadavas
story.timeline_note: After Shishupala's death and the Rajasuya sacrifice, Shalva attacks Dvaraka. Pradyumna leads the defense.
story.comic_potential: High: flying city Saubha, illusion warfare, Pradyumna's dramatic rescue and rebuke.

teachings.teaching: Shalva's vow to destroy the Yadavas arises from pride and humiliation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates how unprocessed emotions (kleshas) lead to harmful actions and karmic entanglement.

teachings.teaching: Shalva's austerity to Shiva shows that even demons can attain boons through tapas, but without inner purification, the boons lead to further downfall.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that true transformation requires not just external practices but inner purification (chitta-shuddhi).

teachings.teaching: Pradyumna's valor and refusal to retreat highlight the importance of dharma and honor in battle.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this represents the warrior's commitment to righteous action without attachment to outcomes (nishkama karma).

teachings.teaching: The charioteer's explanation that protecting a fallen warrior is dharma underscores the principle of situational ethics.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework teaches that dharma is contextual; the charioteer acted out of compassion and duty, not cowardice.

chapter_summary: Krishna battles King Shalva, who uses a magical flying city (Saubha) and illusions. Shalva pretends to have killed Vasudeva, causing Krishna momentary grief, but Krishna realizes the illusion and kills Shalva. The chapter also mentions the aftermath of the Rajasuya sacrifice and Shishupala's death.

story.title: The Slaying of Shalva
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as a warrior king
story.timeline_note: After the Rajasuya sacrifice and Shishupala's death, Krishna returns to Dvaraka and fights Shalva.
story.comic_potential: High: aerial battle with a flying city, illusions, beheading, and divine weapons.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's apparent human emotions (grief, anger) are illusory; his true nature is beyond such limitations.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This illustrates the principle of 'prakriti' (nature) and 'purusha' (consciousness). Krishna's temporary identification with human emotions shows the practitioner how to observe emotions without being bound by them, as the true self is unaffected.

teachings.teaching: One should not be deceived by appearances; discernment (viveka) is essential.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The illusion of Vasudeva's death represents the mind's projections. The practitioner must use discrimination to see through the mind's tricks and realize the unchanging reality.

teachings.teaching: True warriors act, not boast.
